name: chana masala
instructions:
The lighter, tangier chickpea preparation. Distinguished from chole by lemon-ZEST as the tang stack (no black tea, no lemon juice — see notes), more visible tomato, and welcome peppers. Onion form is flexible. ROSEMARY IS A SECRET INGREDIENT (see notes) — non-optional now.

SHARED SPICE BASE: cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, plus lemon-zest tang finish and kasoori methi. NO cardamom in chana masala — that's chole territory. NO amchur either — see notes; amchur is absent from both chickpea variants in this family (chole uses tea, chana masala uses lemon zest). KASHMIRI CHILI POWDER AND GENERIC CHILI POWDER ARE PRIMARY BUT GO IN LAST, TO TASTE — see notes; easy to overdo against the lemon zest.

CHANA MASALA-SPECIFIC SPICE ADDITIONS: ajwain (carom seeds), fennel powder, and dried thyme. Fresh rosemary at the finish. Together these push chana masala further into its mediterranean-adjacent register. Add ajwain and fennel powder to the whole-spice bloom (with the bay leaf); add dried thyme with the ground spices.

DISTINCTIVES:
- Chickpeas as the legume.
- ONIONS: FINELY DICED or PUREED preferred, but chana masala TOLERATES chunky whole onions (unlike chole's strict puree rule). Chunky onions are visible in the finished dish and read more rustic/homestyle.
- WHOLE SPICES in the bloom: bay leaf. NO cardamom.
- NO black tea (that's chole).
- FRESH TOMATOES (chopped or lightly pureed) OR CANNED TOMATOES — both work here as primary; more visible tomato presence than chole.
- Optional small addition of tomato paste for depth (secondary — not the tomato source itself).
- Ghee or vegetable oil both fine.
- LEMON ZEST is the sole tang carrier. NO amchur (that's for the lentil/kidney bean side of the family — see notes). NO lemon juice (see notes).
- NO GREEN PEPPERS — see notes. This variant doesn't take them; heat comes from kashmiri and generic chili powder added late, to taste.
- ROSEMARY, cilantro, and mint all at the finish — the herb trio (fresh rosemary specifically). Kasoori methi too but subordinate.
- NO cinnamon, NO cloves, NO cardamom — those are chole territory (see chole-masala).
- NO OREGANO — see notes; oregano is clashing here now.

PROCEDURE:
1. Heat ghee or oil. Bloom bay leaf, ajwain, and fennel powder briefly.
2. Add onions (finely diced or pureed; chunky tolerated). Cook until soft.
3. Add ginger and garlic. Sauté briefly. (NO fresh green peppers — see notes.)
4. Add ground spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala) plus dried thyme. Toast in the fat. HOLD the kashmiri chili powder and generic chili powder for the end — they go in last, to taste (see notes on chili powder + lemon zest).
5. Add chopped or lightly pureed fresh tomatoes (or canned). Cook down until the fat separates. Small spoon of tomato paste optional here for depth.
6. Add chickpeas (canned or pre-cooked) and enough water to loosen. Simmer to integrate.
7. STIR IN CHILI POWDERS NEAR THE END, TO TASTE: kashmiri chili powder first (for color and gentle heat), then a smaller amount of generic chili powder. Taste after each addition. Stop before it starts to dominate the lemon-zest axis.
8. Finish OFF HEAT with lemon zest (NOT juice), kasoori methi crumbled in, chopped cilantro, torn mint leaves, and a generous scatter of fresh rosemary.
notes:
This recipe used to be Variant B of a master-tomato-dal file. Now standalone.

CHOLE VS CHANA MASALA — WHY THEY'RE SEPARATE RECIPES: chole (see chole-masala) and chana masala are both chickpea dishes but represent genuinely different preparations. Chole leans darker, thicker, more whole-spice-and-tea forward — black tea, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, sometimes anardana for tartness. Chana masala (this recipe) leans lighter, tangier, and lemon-zest-forward, skips the tea, more visible tomato, peppers welcome, mint and rosemary at the finish. In indian home cooking these are often blurred, but the axes above are the real distinction. Pick one direction; don't try to hedge.

CHOLE AND CHANA ALSO REFER TO DIFFERENT CHICKPEA VARIETIES: kabuli chana (large beige, western-supermarket default) vs kala chana (small dark brown/black, indian-grocery specialty). Each has its own preferred dish style. Recipe defaults to kabuli chana.

=== AJWAIN + FENNEL POWDER + DRIED THYME — THE MEDITERRANEAN LEAN ===

Ajwain (carom seeds) and fennel powder both belong in the whole-spice bloom alongside the bay leaf. Ajwain has a thyme-and-oregano-adjacent character with a slight bitter bite; fennel powder adds a soft anise-sweet depth. Together they pair particularly well with chickpeas and reinforce the mediterranean-adjacent identity chana masala leans on. Dried thyme goes in with the ground spices — the herbaceous background that makes the rosemary-at-the-finish (see below) land more cleanly. Without this trio (ajwain + fennel + thyme), the rosemary can read like an outlier addition; with them, the whole herb axis feels intentional.

=== NO OREGANO — CLASHING ===

Oregano used to live here as part of the mediterranean-lean spice trio, but on more testing it doesn't work. The oregano-plus-rosemary combination reads too pizza-parlor / italian-red-sauce for chana masala's tang-and-herb axis; it fights the lemon-zest and pulls the dish toward italian-tomato territory rather than mediterranean-indian. Removed and moved to clashing. Dried thyme fills the same herbaceous slot without the pizza drift.

=== ROSEMARY IS THE SECRET INGREDIENT ===

Rosemary is so good in chana masala I now consider it the secret ingredient of the recipe. Previously listed as want-to-try; the test batch confirmed it. The logic: chana masala's lemon-forward, tomato-visible, peppers-and-mint character is closer to a mediterranean-adjacent indian register than the darker chole. Rosemary's piney depth pairs cleanly with the lemon-zest axis and adds a second-dimension herb note the way it does in mediterranean tomato dishes. Scatter FRESH rosemary generously off heat with the cilantro and mint. Non-optional now — the dish reads noticeably flatter without it. Don't tell anyone this is chana masala's secret; let them think it's the ajwain-fennel-thyme combination doing the work.

=== TANG CARRIER — LEMON ZEST (NOT AMCHUR, NOT LEMON JUICE) ===

CHANA MASALA'S TANG COMES FROM LEMON ZEST ALONE. Amchur (dried mango powder) — often thought of as a chana masala staple — is deliberately absent from this recipe. It belongs on the lentil/kidney bean side of the family (see mediterranean-dal and rajma) where it lands with more flavor weight; here it just muddies the lemon-zest-and-rosemary axis. Lemon zest is the substantially better choice than lemon juice — reaching for the juice when you have the zest is a real downgrade.

Why zest wins over juice so decisively:
- ZEST CONCENTRATES CITRUS OILS WITHOUT WATER: the aromatic oils in the peel carry the bright citrus character while adding zero water to the dish. Chana masala is not a saucy dish and doesn't want to be thinned.
- ZEST'S CITRUS CHARACTER SITS WITH THE ROSEMARY AXIS: the zest's slightly floral, peel-forward aromatics pair cleanly with the rosemary + oregano/thyme + tomato register the recipe was tuned for. Juice's rounder, sweeter, more water-carried acid pushes the dish toward a lemon-butter / mediterranean-pasta register that dilutes the rosemary character.
- ZEST GOES IN OFF HEAT: the zest can be added at the finish alongside cilantro/mint/rosemary without cooking off. Juice tends to want to go in during the simmer, and there its brightness is partially lost to the cook.

Juice is not clashing per se — the dish doesn't fall apart with it — but if you have a lemon, zest it. Don't just squeeze it.

=== TOMATO FORM — FRESH OR CANNED (BOTH PRIMARY), PASTE IS SECONDARY ===

Unlike chole (fresh only) and rajma (paste only), chana masala is flexible on tomato form. Fresh tomatoes AND canned tomatoes are both primary here — pick whichever fits your pantry and mood. Fresh reads brighter and slightly less concentrated; canned reads deeper and more integrated. Tomato paste is welcome as a SECONDARY addition — a small spoonful cooked into the base for depth alongside the fresh/canned tomatoes. Paste alone (no fresh/canned) is rajma territory — don't go there.

=== NO GREEN PEPPERS ===

Green peppers (poblano, jalapeño, serrano, anaheim, bell) are all wrong in chana masala. Chole-masala and mediterranean-dal are the two family variants that take green peppers; chana masala and rajma don't. Reasoning: chana masala's flavor structure is lemon zest + rosemary + fennel + thyme + mint on a fresh/canned tomato base — a tangy, herbal, mediterranean-adjacent register. Green peppers push the dish toward homestyle-curry territory (mediterranean dal's slot) and fight the rosemary-and-lemon-zest identity. Heat comes from kashmiri and generic chili powder stirred in near the end, to taste — not from fresh peppers.

=== MINT AT THE FINISH ===

Mint stirred in raw at the end (with the cilantro and rosemary) works well in chana masala for the same heavy-spice-cabinet reason it works in chole — the spice stack absorbs mint's edge. Don't cook it in.

=== NO CINNAMON, NO CLOVES, NO CARDAMOM ===

All three are chole territory. Adding them here pushes chana masala toward chole and loses the lighter tangier identity. Cardamom is the sneakiest — it's the whole spice most people default to for indian bloom-in-oil work, but it's specifically wrong for chana masala. The floral cardamom character fights the rosemary + lemon zest + ajwain/fennel/thyme herb trio and pushes the dish back toward chole. If you find yourself reaching for any of these, make chole-masala instead.

=== CHILI POWDER IS PRIMARY BUT GOES IN LAST, TO TASTE ===

Kashmiri chili powder and generic chili powder are BOTH PRIMARY in chana masala, same as elsewhere in the family — but the technique is different. Instead of blooming them with the ground spices, hold them back and stir them in near the end of the simmer, to taste. Reasoning: it's easy to overdo chili powder against the lemon-zest + rosemary + fennel + thyme axis. If you add the full amount up front and it turns out to be too much, you can't pull it back — the chili character starts to compete with the citrus and herbs rather than complementing them, and the dish drifts toward a more generic-indian-curry register that dilutes the mediterranean identity.

How to add: kashmiri first (color + gentle heat), taste, then a smaller amount of generic chili powder if you want more depth or bite. Stop before it starts to dominate. Some batches want a lot; some want almost none — the tomato-to-lemon-zest ratio changes what the base can absorb.

New mexico chili powder is also available (secondary) — its smoky-earthy character can pair with the mediterranean lean in small doses (unique in the family for accepting it at all), but again: use sparingly, added at the end. In chole (the tea + cardamom + cloves floral wash doesn't need more earth), mediterranean dal (chunky peppers already carry the vegetal-and-earthy character), and rajma (kashmiri + methi + tomato paste is a locked flavor structure), new mexican wouldn't work at all.

CONTRAST WITH CHOLE (SEE CHOLE-MASALA): chole blooms chili powders with the ground spices up front — they're structural, balancing the tea and cardamom's floral character with a dry warm heat that keeps the dish savory rather than perfumed. Chana masala doesn't have that tea-and-cardamom to balance, so chili powders are a taste-and-adjust ingredient here rather than a build-the-base one.

=== SHARED FAMILY RULES ===

KASHMIRI CHILI POWDER: color and gentle heat without overpowering. Different from generic chili powder. Non-optional across the tomato-dal family (chole, chana masala, mediterranean dal, rajma).

KASOORI METHI: non-optional in this family. Load-bearing flavor, not a garnish. Present in both chana masala AND chole masala at the finish.

MINT AT THE FINISH — CANONICAL IN BOTH CHICKPEA VARIANTS: fresh mint stirred in raw at the end works in both chana masala and chole masala. In chana masala, mint sits with the rosemary + cilantro + lemon zest trio at the finish; in chole masala, mint pairs with cloves and the tea+cardamom floral wash (see the mint-and-cloves note in chole-masala). Different rationale each variant, same finishing move.

SIBLING RECIPES:
- chole-masala: the darker, thicker, more aromatic chickpea preparation with black tea + cardamom + cloves + cinnamon.
- mediterranean-dal: the chunky lentil dal — dry lentils into the pot, lots of chunky peppers and onions. The most 'curry' of the family.
- rajma: the kidney bean gravy — tomato paste concentrated, ghee non-optional, methi-forward.
